title: HashMaps
description: Master HashMap operations essential for coding interviews: put/get, containsKey, getOrDefault, iteration, and frequency counting.

sections.title: HashMap Basics: put, get, containsKey
sections.explanation: HashMap<K,V> stores key-value pairs with O(1) average lookup. Create with `new HashMap<>()`. Use `put(key, val)` to insert/update, `get(key)` to retrieve (returns null if missing), `containsKey(key)` to check existence. Keys must be immutable (String, Integer). Import `java.util.HashMap`.

exercise.prompt: Create a HashMap<String, String> mapping country codes to names: "US"->"United States", "UK"->"United Kingdom", "JP"->"Japan". Print the value for "UK", whether "FR" exists as a key, and the size of the map.

validation.message: UK maps to United Kingdom, FR doesn't exist, size is 3.
Use put(), get(), and containsKey() methods.

exercise.hint: put each code-name pair. get("UK") returns the name. containsKey("FR") returns false.

sections.title: getOrDefault and Updating Values
sections.explanation: `getOrDefault(key, defaultValue)` returns the value for key if present, otherwise returns defaultValue. This is cleaner than checking containsKey first. Common pattern for counting: `map.put(key, map.getOrDefault(key, 0) + 1)`. Also useful: `putIfAbsent(key, val)` only inserts if key is missing, `merge(key, val, remappingFn)` for combining values.

exercise.prompt: Create a HashMap<String, Integer> to count word occurrences. For the words ["apple", "banana", "apple", "cherry", "banana", "apple"], count each using getOrDefault. Print the count for "apple", "banana", and "cherry".

validation.message: Use getOrDefault to handle missing keys when counting.

sections.title: Iterating Over a HashMap
sections.explanation: Three ways to iterate: `map.keySet()` for keys only, `map.values()` for values only, `map.entrySet()` for key-value pairs. Use `Map.Entry<K,V>` with `entry.getKey()` and `entry.getValue()`. Import `java.util.Map`. EntrySet is the most efficient for accessing both key and value. Order is not guaranteed in HashMap; use LinkedHashMap for insertion order or TreeMap for sorted order.

exercise.prompt: Create a HashMap mapping "math"->95, "science"->87, "english"->92. Use a for loop over entrySet() to find and print the subject with the highest score. Then print the sum of all scores using values().

validation.message: Use entrySet() with getKey()/getValue() and values() for iteration.

exercise.hint: Track bestSubject and bestScore. Compare entry.getValue() against bestScore in each iteration.

sections.title: Frequency Counting Pattern
sections.explanation: The frequency map is one of the most common interview patterns. Build a character or element frequency map, then query it. Steps: (1) iterate input, (2) count with getOrDefault, (3) process the map. Used in anagram detection, top-K frequent elements, first unique character, and more. Time: O(n) to build, O(1) per lookup.

exercise.prompt: Given int[] nums = {1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 3, 3, 4}, build a frequency map. Print the frequency of 3. Then find and print the element that appears exactly once (the unique element).

validation.message: 3 appears 3 times. 4 is the only element appearing once.
Use getOrDefault to build the frequency map and get() to query it.

exercise.hint: Build freq with getOrDefault pattern. Then iterate entrySet to find the entry with value == 1.

sections.title: HashMap for Two Sum Pattern
sections.explanation: The classic Two Sum: find two numbers that add to a target. Use a HashMap to store {value: index} as you iterate. For each element, check if `target - current` exists in the map. If yes, you found the pair. If no, add current to the map. This gives O(n) time instead of O(n^2) brute force. This pattern extends to many "find complement" problems.

exercise.prompt: Implement a static method `twoSum(int[] nums, int target)` that returns the indices of two numbers adding to target. Use a HashMap for O(n) solution. Test with nums={3, 2, 4} target=6, print the two indices separated by a comma.

validation.message: nums[1]=2 and nums[2]=4 sum to 6, so output is 1,2.
Check for complement with containsKey, compute target - nums[i], and store in map.

exercise.hint: For each i: complement = target - nums[i]. If map.containsKey(complement), return {map.get(complement), i}. Else put(nums[i], i).
